sectionName: Biological & Biochemical Foundations of Living Systems

questions.passage: Researchers studied the population dynamics of two competing rodent species—Species A, a habitat generalist, and Species B, a seed specialist—in a semi-arid grassland over 15 years. A prolonged drought occurred during years 6–9, reducing total seed availability by 70%. After the drought ended in year 10, seed availability recovered within two years. Dietary analysis showed that Species A shifted substantially toward insect prey during the drought and retained substantial insect consumption even after seed recovery, while Species B returned to exclusive seed consumption. The population densities for both species over the 15-year period are shown in Figure 1.
questions.stem: The ability of Species A to switch from seeds to insects during the drought is best described as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: character displacement driven by the presence of Species B

choices.label: B
choices.text: a fundamental niche shift caused by genetic adaptation

choices.label: C
choices.text: behavioral plasticity allowing exploitation of an alternative resource

choices.label: D
choices.text: Müllerian mimicry of insectivorous species in the community

explanations.A: Character displacement refers to evolved morphological or behavioral divergence between competing species in sympatry versus allopatry, occurring over evolutionary timescales. The dietary shift described occurred within the generation time of the study (years, not generations of selection) and represents behavioral flexibility, not evolved character divergence driven by Species B's presence.
explanations.B: A fundamental niche describes the total range of conditions and resources an organism can theoretically exploit in the absence of biotic interactions. The dietary shift to insects does not represent a change in fundamental niche, which would require genetic change. Instead, Species A is expressing a portion of its existing fundamental niche (insectivory) that was previously unexploited, reflecting behavioral plasticity rather than genetic adaptation.
explanations.C: Correct. Species A is a habitat generalist with the behavioral flexibility to exploit alternative food resources when preferred resources become scarce. This dietary switching from seeds to insects represents phenotypic plasticity in foraging behavior—the ability to modify resource use without genetic change. This plasticity is characteristic of generalist species and allows persistence during environmental perturbations that would eliminate specialists dependent on a single resource type.
explanations.D: Müllerian mimicry occurs when two or more unpalatable species evolve similar warning signals, mutually reinforcing predator avoidance learning. This is a defensive adaptation against predation and has no relevance to dietary flexibility or resource switching during drought conditions.

questions.stem: Which ecological concept most comprehensively explains the divergent trajectories of Species A and Species B observed in Figure 1 between years 10 and 15?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Character displacement driven by sympatric speciation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Density-independent regulation due to abiotic environmental factors

choices.label: C
choices.text: Competitive exclusion restricting the realized niche of Species A

choices.label: D
choices.text: Secondary succession leading to a higher carrying capacity for Species B

explanations.A: Character displacement refers to evolved divergence between competing species, usually in morphology or resource use, over evolutionary timescales. The post-drought divergence in Figure 1 occurs over only a few years and is tied to immediate ecological competition after resource recovery, not sympatric speciation or inherited trait divergence.
explanations.B: Density-independent regulation describes effects of abiotic disturbances such as drought that affect populations regardless of density. The shaded drought period explains the crash during years 6–9, but the question asks about years 10–15 after the drought has ended. The continued suppression of Species A while Species B recovers is better explained by a biotic interaction: competition for the recovered seed resource.
explanations.C: Correct. Figure 1 shows Species B returning to approximately its pre-drought density by years 13–15, while Species A remains far below its original density despite seed recovery. Because Species A retained insect consumption while Species B resumed exclusive seed use, the most comprehensive explanation is that Species B competitively restricted Species A's realized niche, preventing Species A from returning to its pre-drought seed-heavy resource use.
explanations.D: Secondary succession would involve a directional change in the plant or community structure after disturbance. The passage describes a semi-arid grassland with seed availability recovering after drought, not replacement of the community through succession. Species B also returns roughly to its pre-drought density rather than reaching a newly higher carrying capacity.

questions.stem: Based on the passage data, the carrying capacity (K) for Species B during the drought years was most directly reduced by:

choices.label: A
choices.text: decreased immigration from neighboring populations

choices.label: B
choices.text: increased predation from drought-stressed raptors

choices.label: C
choices.text: reduced availability of its limiting resource (seeds)

choices.label: D
choices.text: density-dependent disease transmission within the population

explanations.A: While immigration can influence population size, the passage does not discuss metapopulation dynamics or immigration from external populations. The carrying capacity is determined by local resource availability within the study plots, and the passage explicitly identifies the 70% reduction in seed availability as the environmental change during the drought.
explanations.B: The passage provides no information about predation pressure from raptors or any other predators. Attributing the population decline to predation introduces an unsupported variable when the passage directly states that seed availability dropped by 70%, providing a sufficient explanation.
explanations.C: Correct. Carrying capacity is the maximum population size that an environment can sustain indefinitely given available resources. For Species B, a seed specialist, the primary limiting resource is seed availability. The 70% reduction in total seed availability during the drought directly reduced the carrying capacity for Species B, explaining why this specialist declined sharply—it had no alternative food source to buffer against seed loss, unlike the generalist Species A.
explanations.D: Density-dependent disease transmission increases as population density rises and would not explain a decline caused by resource reduction. Species B's decline occurred because of external environmental change (drought reducing seeds), not because high population density facilitated pathogen spread. Additionally, disease would be less likely as the declining population became sparser.

questions.stem: A graduate student proposes modeling the Species A–Species B interaction using the Lotka-Volterra competition equations. In this model, the competition coefficient α₁₂ represents:

choices.label: A
choices.text: the intrinsic growth rate of Species A in the absence of Species B

choices.label: B
choices.text: the effect of one individual of Species B on the population growth rate of Species A

choices.label: C
choices.text: the probability that Species A will go extinct during competitive interactions

choices.label: D
choices.text: the mutation rate generating novel dietary adaptations in Species A

explanations.A: The intrinsic growth rate (r) is a separate parameter in the Lotka-Volterra model representing the maximum per-capita rate of population increase when resources are unlimited and no competitors are present. Competition coefficients specifically quantify the per-capita competitive effect of one species on another, not growth in competitor absence.
explanations.B: Correct. In the Lotka-Volterra competition model, α₁₂ (alpha-one-two) converts individuals of Species 2 (here, Species B) into competitive equivalents of Species 1 (Species A). It quantifies how much each individual of Species B reduces the growth rate of Species A relative to intraspecific competition. If α₁₂ = 0.5, each Species B individual has half the competitive effect on Species A as another Species A individual would. This parameterization allows the model to capture asymmetric competition between species.
explanations.C: Extinction probability is not directly parameterized in the deterministic Lotka-Volterra model. The outcome of competition (coexistence or exclusion) is determined by the relationship between competition coefficients and carrying capacities (K₁/α₁₂ versus K₂ and K₂/α₂₁ versus K₁), not by a single coefficient representing extinction probability.
explanations.D: Mutation rates are not part of the ecological Lotka-Volterra competition framework. This model operates on ecological timescales assuming fixed species traits. Evolutionary models incorporating mutation and selection (e.g., adaptive dynamics) are separate frameworks not described in the standard Lotka-Volterra competition equations.

questions.stem: If a third species, Species C (a strict insectivore), were introduced to the study plots after year 10, which prediction is most consistent with the passage findings?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Species A would be most negatively affected, as it now faces competitors for both seeds and insects

choices.label: B
choices.text: Species B would be most negatively affected due to its narrow dietary niche

choices.label: C
choices.text: Species C would immediately outcompete both residents through greater overall fitness

choices.label: D
choices.text: All three species would coexist at pre-drought densities through resource partitioning

explanations.A: Correct. After the drought, Species A retained substantial insect consumption while also competing with Species B for seeds. Introducing Species C (a strict insectivore) would create competition for the insect resource that Species A had shifted toward. Species A would then face competitive pressure on both of its food resources: seeds (from Species B) and insects (from Species C). This two-front competition would most severely impact the generalist Species A, potentially squeezing it from both ends of its realized niche.
explanations.B: Species B's narrow dietary niche (seed specialist) would not be directly affected by the introduction of an insectivore. Species C eats insects, not seeds, so there is no direct resource overlap with Species B. Species B already demonstrated rapid recovery after the drought when seed availability returned, and its populations would not be directly impacted by insect competition.
explanations.C: Competitive superiority cannot be assumed simply from introduction. Species C's success would depend on its competitive ability relative to Species A for insect resources specifically. The passage provides no basis for predicting that Species C would outcompete both residents, especially since it shares no dietary overlap with Species B.
explanations.D: Complete coexistence at pre-drought densities is unlikely because Species A is already suppressed below pre-drought levels by competition with Species B. Adding a third competitor for Species A's alternative resource (insects) would further suppress Species A, not restore all species to pre-drought densities. Resource partitioning reduces competition but does not guarantee pre-disturbance population levels.

questions.passage: Marine biologists monitored coral reef fish communities on two adjacent reef systems in the Caribbean over a decade. Reef 1 experienced a mass bleaching event in year 3 that killed 80% of live coral cover. Reef 2 was protected by deeper water and cooler upwelling currents, retaining 90% coral cover throughout the study. Pre-bleaching surveys documented 47 fish species on Reef 1 and 45 species on Reef 2, with similar species compositions. Post-bleaching surveys on Reef 1 showed a progressive decline in species richness to 28 species by year 7, with herbivorous and corallivorous guilds declining most sharply. However, planktivorous species increased in abundance on Reef 1. By year 10, Reef 1 showed partial coral recovery (35% live cover), and fish species richness had rebounded to 36 species. Throughout the study, Reef 2 maintained stable species richness between 43 and 47 species.
questions.stem: The decline in species richness on Reef 1 after the bleaching event is most directly explained by:

choices.label: A
choices.text: genetic drift reducing allelic diversity within small fish populations

choices.label: B
choices.text: introduction of invasive predators coinciding with the bleaching event

choices.label: C
choices.text: increased salinity from evaporation of shallow reef water

choices.label: D
choices.text: loss of habitat complexity and structural refugia provided by live coral

explanations.A: Genetic drift acts on allele frequencies within populations over generations and would not directly cause the rapid loss of entire species from the reef community within four years. The observed pattern is an ecological community shift driven by habitat loss, not a population genetics phenomenon operating at the allelic level.
explanations.B: The passage does not mention any invasive predator introduction. Attributing the species decline to invasive predators introduces an unsupported variable when the bleaching event and habitat loss provide a complete, well-documented mechanistic explanation for the observed community changes.
explanations.C: Salinity changes from evaporation are not a documented consequence of coral bleaching events. Bleaching is caused by elevated sea surface temperatures disrupting the coral-zooxanthellae symbiosis. The passage attributes the bleaching to thermal stress (Reef 2 was protected by cooler upwelling), not salinity changes.
explanations.D: Correct. Live coral provides the three-dimensional structural complexity that supports reef fish diversity: crevices for shelter from predators, territories for breeding, and microhabitats for specialized feeders. When 80% of live coral was killed by bleaching, the physical habitat structure collapsed. Obligate coral-dwelling species (corallivores feeding on coral polyps, small species using coral branches for refuge) lost their essential habitat, explaining why these guilds declined most sharply. The pattern of herbivore decline also fits: dead coral becomes overgrown by algae, initially seeming favorable for herbivores, but the loss of structural refuge from predation ultimately reduces herbivore persistence.

questions.stem: The increase in planktivorous fish abundance on Reef 1 after bleaching is an example of:

choices.label: A
choices.text: primary succession by pioneer species on a newly formed substrate

choices.label: B
choices.text: competitive release, as reduced competition from declining guilds freed resources

choices.label: C
choices.text: symbiosis between planktivores and the recovering coral colonies

choices.label: D
choices.text: directional selection favoring planktivory as a feeding strategy

explanations.A: Primary succession occurs on newly formed, barren substrates (volcanic rock, glacial till) without prior biological communities. The bleached reef is a disturbed existing community—this would be secondary succession at best. More importantly, the planktivore increase reflects a shift in community composition, not the sequential colonization pattern that defines ecological succession.
explanations.B: Correct. Competitive release occurs when a species increases in abundance after the decline or removal of a competitor. As corallivorous and herbivorous guilds declined due to coral loss, the ecological resources and space previously occupied or contested by these guilds became available. Planktivores, which feed in the water column and are less dependent on coral structure, could expand their populations by exploiting reduced competition for reef territory, resting sites, and other shared resources.
explanations.C: The passage does not describe any mutualistic interaction between planktivores and coral. Planktivores feed on zooplankton in the water column and do not have a direct symbiotic relationship with coral colonies. Some reef fish do provide nutrient subsidies to corals through fecal deposition, but this is not the mechanism driving the population increase described.
explanations.D: Directional selection operates on heritable genetic variation over multiple generations, favoring individuals with traits that confer fitness advantages. The planktivore increase occurred within a few years—too rapid for evolutionary change in fish populations with generation times of 1–5 years. This is an ecological community shift (population-level change in abundance), not an evolutionary adaptation in feeding strategy.

questions.stem: Using the Shannon diversity index (H' = -Σpᵢ ln pᵢ), a researcher calculates diversity for both reefs. Compared to species richness alone, the Shannon index additionally accounts for:

choices.label: A
choices.text: the geographic range of each species across the reef

choices.label: B
choices.text: the trophic level of each species in the food web

choices.label: C
choices.text: the phylogenetic relationships among species

choices.label: D
choices.text: the relative abundance (evenness) of species in the community

explanations.A: Geographic range (the spatial distribution of a species across the reef) is not incorporated into the Shannon diversity index. The index uses proportional abundance (pᵢ) of each species in a sample, regardless of how that species is spatially distributed across the habitat.
explanations.B: Trophic level is an ecosystem ecology concept describing a species' position in the food web. The Shannon index treats all species equivalently regardless of their ecological role, trophic position, body size, or functional traits. Incorporating trophic structure would require a different analytical framework.
explanations.C: Phylogenetic diversity indices (e.g., Faith's PD) incorporate evolutionary relationships among species by summing branch lengths on a phylogenetic tree. The Shannon index does not consider phylogenetic relatedness—it treats all species as equivalent units distinguished only by their relative abundances.
explanations.D: Correct. Species richness simply counts the number of species present without considering how individuals are distributed among those species. The Shannon index incorporates both richness and evenness through the proportional abundance term (pᵢ). A community with 10 equally abundant species has higher Shannon diversity than a community with 10 species where one species comprises 91% of individuals. The -Σpᵢ ln pᵢ formula reaches its maximum value when all species are equally abundant (maximum evenness) and decreases as the distribution becomes more uneven.

questions.stem: The partial species recovery on Reef 1 by year 10 (36 of original 47 species) alongside partial coral recovery (35% live cover) best illustrates:

choices.label: A
choices.text: convergent evolution of reef-adapted traits in recolonizing fish

choices.label: B
choices.text: the founder effect determining which species recolonized the reef

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium being re-established in the fish community

choices.label: D
choices.text: a positive correlation between habitat structural complexity and species richness

explanations.A: Convergent evolution requires independent lineages evolving similar traits in response to similar selective pressures over evolutionary timescales. The fish recolonizing Reef 1 are the same species returning to recovering habitat, not different lineages independently evolving reef-adapted traits. The timescale (years) is also far too short for evolutionary convergence.
explanations.B: The founder effect describes the reduced genetic diversity that occurs when a new population is established by a small number of individuals. While the species recolonizing Reef 1 may come from small founding populations, the founder effect is a population genetics concept about allele frequencies, not a community ecology principle explaining which species return to a recovering habitat.
explanations.C: Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium describes conditions maintaining allele frequencies in a population (no selection, no drift, no migration, random mating, no mutation). It is a population genetics concept applied to single populations, not a community ecology pattern. It cannot explain community-level species richness changes on a reef.
explanations.D: Correct. The parallel recovery of coral cover and fish species richness directly supports the species-area/habitat relationship: as structural habitat (live coral) increases, the number of species the environment can support also increases. The incomplete recovery (35% coral → 36/47 species) is consistent with this correlation—partial habitat restoration supports partial biodiversity recovery but not full restoration. This pattern is foundational to conservation biology and predicts that full species recovery requires substantial habitat restoration.

questions.stem: A conservation biologist proposes that Reef 2 could serve as a source population for recolonization of Reef 1. This strategy is based on the ecological concept of:

choices.label: A
choices.text: metapopulation dynamics, where connected subpopulations sustain regional persistence

choices.label: B
choices.text: the competitive exclusion principle, where one species eliminates another

choices.label: C
choices.text: disruptive selection favoring extreme phenotypes in the population

choices.label: D
choices.text: endosymbiosis transferring organelles between reef organisms

explanations.A: Correct. Metapopulation theory (Levins) describes how spatially separated subpopulations connected by dispersal can maintain regional species persistence even when individual subpopulations experience local extinction. Reef 2 maintaining stable, high diversity makes it a source population—a subpopulation with positive growth that exports individuals via dispersal. Reef 1, having lost species, functions as a sink that could be rescued through immigration from Reef 2. This source-sink dynamic is a core metapopulation concept applied widely in conservation planning.
explanations.B: The competitive exclusion principle (Gause) states that two species competing for the same limiting resource cannot stably coexist—one will exclude the other. This principle addresses interspecific competition outcomes, not the spatial dynamics of recolonization from source to sink populations across habitat patches.
explanations.C: Disruptive selection is an evolutionary mechanism favoring extreme phenotypes at both ends of a trait distribution over intermediate phenotypes. It is a population genetics concept unrelated to conservation strategies involving spatial connectivity between reef populations.
explanations.D: Endosymbiosis is the evolutionary process by which one organism lives inside another, as in the origin of mitochondria and chloroplasts from bacterial endosymbionts. This is a cell biology concept with no relevance to population-level conservation strategies for reef fish recolonization.
